title: Multi-Tenancy & Isolation in SAP
description: Multi-tenancy is the architectural backbone of SaaS platforms. This module covers tenant isolation models (schema-per-tenant, database-per-tenant, pool-per-tenant), S/4HANA Cloud multi-tenancy architecture, BTP subscription management and HDI containers for tenant-aware development, CDS DCL for multi-tenant row-level security and data isolation, cross-tenant analytics patterns for consolidated reporting, and multi-cloud SAP deployment on AWS/GCP/Azure with data sovereignty considerations. Staff+ engineers design tenant isolation that balances security, cost, and operational complexity.

sections.title: Tenant Isolation Models — Schema, DB & Pool Strategies

description.value:
Tenant isolation is the most fundamental architectural decision for any multi-tenant SaaS platform. The choice of isolation model determines the maximum number of tenants you can support, the per-tenant cost, the operational complexity of tenant management, and — most critically — the security guarantees you can offer. For SAP platforms (S/4HANA Cloud, BTP, SAP SuccessFactors), each isolation model has been deployed at scale, and the Staff+ architect must understand the trade-offs to select the right model for each workload type.

Database per Tenant (Isolated): Each tenant has its own HANA database instance (or schema within a multi-tenant database container). This provides the strongest isolation — a tenant's data is physically separated from other tenants. Performance isolation is excellent: one tenant's heavy query cannot impact another tenant's response time. Backup, restore, and disaster recovery are per-tenant, so you can restore one tenant without affecting others. The cost, however, is high: each database has overhead (memory allocation, connection pool, backup storage). This model is suitable for large enterprise tenants with hundreds of users, strict SLA requirements, and regulatory data isolation mandates (e.g., financial services, healthcare). SAP SuccessFactors uses this model — each customer gets a dedicated database schema. The maximum tenant count is limited by database server capacity (typically hundreds to low thousands of tenants per server).

Schema per Tenant: All tenants share a single database instance, but each tenant's data is isolated in a separate schema. The schemas share the same database process and memory, reducing per-tenant overhead. Schema-per-tenant is the most common model in BTP multi-tenant applications because it balances isolation with cost. The HANA multi-tenant database containers (MDC) support up to 40,000 tenants per HANA instance by default. Backup and restore can be per-schema or for the entire database — per-schema restore enables tenant-level recovery without affecting others. Performance isolation is weaker than DB-per-tenant — one tenant's resource-intensive query can consume database CPU and I/O, affecting other tenants. Mitigation: use HANA workload management (resource pools) to limit each tenant's CPU and memory consumption.

Pool per Tenant (Shared): All tenants share both the database instance and the schema — tenant identity is a column in every table (e.g., tenant_id in every row). This is the most cost-efficient model (lowest per-tenant overhead) but provides the weakest isolation: data separation depends entirely on correct application logic (e.g., all queries must include WHERE tenant_id = ?). A missing WHERE clause exposes one tenant's data to all tenants. Performance isolation is essentially non-existent — any tenant can cause resource contention. This model is suitable for low-value transactions with many small tenants (e.g., a SaaS app for small businesses with thousands of tenants, each with fewer than 10 users). It is the riskiest model and should be used only when the application layer has rigorous, tested, and default-enforced tenant isolation.

* HANA Multi-Tenant Database Containers (MDC)
* List all tenants in a HANA MDC system:
SELECT * FROM SYS_DATABASES.M_SERVICES
  WHERE SERVICE_NAME = 'indexserver';

* Create a new tenant database (schema-per-tenant):
CREATE DATABASE TENANT_ACME
  SYSTEM USER PASSWORD 'initial_pw';

* Alter tenant database resource limits
ALTER DATABASE TENANT_ACME
  DISK SIZE = 100 GB
  MEMORY SIZE = 4 GB
  CPU CORE LIMIT = 2;

* Backup a single tenant
BACKUP DATA FOR TENANT_ACME
  USING FILE ('/backup/tenant_acme');

* Recovery of a single tenant
RECOVER DATA FOR TENANT_ACME
  USING FILE ('/backup/tenant_acme')
  CLEAR LOG;

* ABAP: tenant-aware connection handling
* In BTP multi-tenant ABAP Environment:
* The tenant ID is available via environment variable
DATA lv_tenant_id TYPE string.
lv_tenant_id = cl_abap_typedescr=>get_environment(
  )->get_parameter( 'TENANT_ID' ).

* For pool-per-tenant: all queries must include tenant_id
* BAD: SELECT * FROM zorders (exposes all tenants)
* GOOD:
SELECT * FROM zorders
  WHERE tenant_id = @lv_tenant_id.

* Application layer enforcement:
* Use CDS view with a DCL role that filters by tenant_id
* Then any query against the CDS view is automatically isolated

decisionTree.title: Choosing the Right Tenant Isolation Model
decisionTree.prompt: Should I use database-per-tenant, schema-per-tenant, or pool-per-tenant for this multi-tenant SaaS application?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "What are the data isolation and SLA requirements per tenant?" If tenants require physical data separation for compliance (financial services, healthcare), use database-per-tenant. If tenants are medium-sized with standard SLAs, use schema-per-tenant. If tenants are small and numerous with shared SLAs, use pool-per-tenant.

branches.condition: Regulatory compliance requires physical data separation (HIPAA, PCI DSS, FedRAMP)
branches.path: Database-per-tenant. Each tenant gets a dedicated HANA MDC database. Strongest isolation. Per-tenant backup/restore. Cost: high — each tenant consumes minimum 2GB memory + CPU overhead. Max: 100s of tenants per server.

branches.condition: Medium tenants (50-500 users), standard SLAs, cost-sensitive
branches.path: Schema-per-tenant. All tenants share one HANA MDC instance, each in its own schema. Balanced isolation and cost. Up to 40K tenants per instance. DCL enforces row-level security. Best for: BTP multi-tenant apps, S/4HANA Cloud.

branches.condition: Small tenants (< 50 users), high volume, cost-optimized
branches.path: Pool-per-tenant. All data in shared tables with tenant_id column. Lowest cost. DCL is critical for security. Max: millions of tenants. Best for: consumer-scale SaaS, low-touch onboarding.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The Staff+ answer is never a single model. Use a tiered model: large enterprise tenants get database-per-tenant, mid-market gets schema-per-tenant, SMB gets pool-per-tenant. The tenant tier is determined at subscription time and can be upgraded later. This is how Salesforce, ServiceNow, and Workday structure their multi-tenant architectures.

description.value:
S/4HANA Cloud (public edition) is SAP's flagship SaaS product, and its multi-tenancy architecture is the result of years of refinement. Unlike on-premise S/4HANA where each customer gets a dedicated system, S/4HANA Cloud uses a tenant-per-customer model within shared infrastructure. Understanding this architecture is critical for Staff+ engineers who design BTP extensions that must integrate with S/4HANA Cloud tenants.

Architecture Layers: S/4HANA Cloud uses a three-layer multi-tenancy architecture: (1) Infrastructure Layer — shared compute clusters (Kubernetes-based) and HANA MDC database servers. The infrastructure is multi-tenant, but a single tenant's workloads are scheduled across multiple nodes in the cluster. A compromised tenant cannot access another tenant's processes due to Linux namespace isolation and container-level security. (2) Application Layer — each tenant has a dedicated ABAP application server process group within the shared ABAP Platform instance. The ABAP runtime is tenant-aware: the tenant ID is part of the user session, and all authorization checks include tenant context. The ABAP dispatcher routes requests to the correct tenant's work processes. (3) Database Layer — each tenant has a dedicated HANA MDC schema (schema-per-tenant model). The schema is created during tenant provisioning and contains the tenant's complete data and metadata. This provides strong data isolation at the database level.

Tenant Provisioning: A new S/4HANA Cloud tenant is provisioned using a template-based approach: (1) A master template (containing the standard S/4HANA Cloud schemas) is replicated to create a new tenant schema. (2) The schema is pre-populated with standard configuration (company code, chart of accounts, organizational structure defaults for the customer's region/industry). (3) The customer's customizations (defined in the scope wizard) are applied. (4) The tenant is activated and the customer receives the URL. The entire process takes between 24 and 72 hours for standard tenants. The Staff+ consideration is the tenant lifecycle: provisioning automation, de-provisioning (data retention and secure deletion per GDPR), tenant suspension (for non-payment), and tenant upgrade (apply quarterly stack across all tenants simultaneously or in a staged rollout).

API Access and Extensions: S/4HANA Cloud provides RESTful APIs (OData V4) and events for BTP extension applications. The extension application receives the tenant context in the JWT token (via XSUAA), which includes the tenant ID. The extension must use this tenant ID to scope all API calls to the correct S/4HANA Cloud tenant. This is the tenant-aware extension pattern: the extension itself must be either multi-tenant (understanding multiple S/4HANA tenants) or per-tenant (one extension instance per S/4HANA tenant). The multi-tenant extension is more cost-efficient but requires rigorous tenant isolation in the extension's own data store.

* S/4HANA Cloud tenant context from JWT token
* In BTP ABAP Environment, get tenant ID:

CLASS zcl_cloud_tenant_context DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    CLASS-METHODS: get_tenant_id
      RETURNING VALUE(rv_tenant_id) TYPE string,
      get_subdomain
        RETURNING VALUE(rv_subdomain) TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_cloud_tenant_context IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_tenant_id.
    " In BTP, tenant ID is in the JWT token
    DATA(lo_request) = cl_http_request=>get_request( ).
    DATA(lv_jwt) = lo_request->get_header_field(
      '~request_header' )->get_value(
      'Authorization' ).

    " Decode JWT to extract tenant GUID
    " JWT payload contains 'zid' (tenant ID) claim
    " Format: https://<tenant>.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_subdomain.
    rv_subdomain = cl_abap_typedescr=>get_environment(
      )->get_parameter( 'CF_INSTANCE_GUID' ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Multi-tenant extension data isolation
* Create a table with tenant_id as first key field

@EndUserText.label: 'Multi-tenant extension data'
@AbapCatalog.enhancementCategory: #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
define table zext_data {
  key client    : mandt not null;
  key tenant_id : string not null;
  key record_id : char32 not null;
      payload   : string;
      created_at: utclong;
      created_by: uname;
}

* Always filter by tenant_id:
SELECT * FROM zext_data
  WHERE tenant_id = @lv_tenant_id
  INTO TABLE @DATA(lt_tenant_data).

* S/4HANA Cloud API call with tenant context
* OData request:
* GET /sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_salesorder/srvd_a2x/sap/salesorder/0001/
* Header: Authorization: Bearer <jwt_with_tenant_context>
* The S/4HANA Cloud gateway resolves the tenant from the JWT

BTP (SAP Business Technology Platform) provides the infrastructure for building, deploying, and managing multi-tenant SaaS applications. The two key technologies for multi-tenancy on BTP are the SaaS Provisioning Service (for subscription management — tenant onboarding, offboarding, and lifecycle) and HDI (HANA Deployment Infrastructure) Containers (for database-level tenant isolation in CAP and XS Advanced applications). A Staff+ engineer must understand both to design a complete multi-tenant application on BTP.

SaaS Provisioning Service: The SaaS Provisioning Service manages the lifecycle of multi-tenant applications on BTP. When a customer subscribes to your application, the service: (1) creates a new tenant in the application's tenant registry, (2) provisions the required infrastructure (HDI container, application route, service bindings), (3) triggers the application's tenant onboarding callback (a URL you define that initializes the tenant's data), and (4) returns the subscription URL to the customer. The application's tenant registry is a simple database table mapping tenant ID to tenant configuration (subdomain, region, plan, status). The SaaS Provisioning Service also handles offboarding: when a subscription is canceled, it triggers the tenant deletion callback, and you must clean up all tenant data (including HDI containers, application data, and integration configurations).

HDI Containers: HDI containers are the HANA database's mechanism for tenant isolation in multi-tenant CAP and XS Advanced applications. Each tenant gets a dedicated HDI container, which is a set of database schemas with deployment artifacts (tables, views, procedures) isolated from other tenants. HDI containers provide: (1) Development-time isolation: each container has its own version of the application artifacts — the container is a self-contained deployment unit. (2) Runtime isolation: each container is a separate database schema with separate connection pools, resource allocation, and security context. (3) Lifecycle isolation: you can upgrade each tenant's HDI container independently — this is critical for staged rollouts. HDI containers are managed via the HDI Container API (REST) and the HDI_ADMIN SQL role. The container group is a logical grouping of containers that share resource limits.

Tenant-Aware Routing in BTP: The Application Router (approuter) in BTP Cloud Foundry handles tenant-aware routing. It intercepts incoming requests, extracts the tenant from the URL subdomain or from the JWT token, and routes the request to the correct application instance with the tenant context. The approuter also handles tenant-specific configurations (UI theme, branding, language). For CAP applications, the @cap.tenant annotation enables automatic tenant context propagation from the approuter to the CAP service.

Subscription</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="110" y="170">Customer subscribes</text><rect class="sys-box" x="200" y="140" width="140" height="40"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="270" y="155">2. Provision</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="270" y="170">Create HDI container</text><rect class="sys-box" x="360" y="140" width="140" height="40"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="430" y="155">3. Activate</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="590" y="170">Subscription URL ready</text><line x1="180" y1="160" x2="200" y2="160" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="340" y1="160" x2="360" y2="160" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="500" y1="160" x2="520" y2="160" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="180" x2="350" y2="210" class="sys-edge"/><rect class="hi-box" x="40" y="210" width="620" height="40"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="230">Application Router — Tenant-Aware Request Routing</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="245">Subdomain -> Tenant ID -> Backend route -> JWT propagation</text><line x1="130" y1="250" x2="130" y2="275" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="250" x2="350" y2="275" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="570" y1="250" x2="570" y2="275" class="sys-edge"/><text class="sys-lbl" x="130" y="293">HDI Container A</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="130" y="308">Tenant A schema</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="293">HDI Container B</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="308">Tenant B schema</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" y="293">HDI Container C</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="570" y="308">Tenant C schema</text><line x1="130" y1="315" x2="130" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="350" y1="315" x2="350" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><line x1="570" y1="315" x2="570" y2="340" class="sys-edge"/><text class="hi-lbl" x="350" y="370">Staged upgrade: upgrade 10% of tenants -> monitor -> roll out</text></svg>

* BTP SaaS Provisioning — tenant callback handler
* The application exposes a callback URL for tenant lifecycle events
* Endpoint: POST /callback/v1/tenants/{tenantId}

CLASS zcl_saas_tenant_handler DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: on_subscribe
      IMPORTING
        iv_tenant_id   TYPE string
        iv_subdomain   TYPE string
        iv_plan        TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_status) TYPE string,
      on_unsubscribe
        IMPORTING iv_tenant_id TYPE string.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_saas_tenant_handler IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD on_subscribe.
    " 1. Register tenant in local registry
    INSERT INTO ztenant_registry VALUES (
      tenant_id  = iv_tenant_id
      subdomain  = iv_subdomain
      plan       = iv_plan
      status     = 'PROVISIONING'
      created_at = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
    ).

    " 2. Create HDI container via SQL
    " Requires EXECUTE IMMEDIATE with HDI_ADMIN role
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE |CREATE HDI CONTAINER { iv_tenant_id }|.

    " 3. Deploy tenant artifacts to container
    " Call HDI deploy API

    " 4. Initialize tenant configuration data

    " 5. Mark tenant as active
    UPDATE ztenant_registry SET status = 'ACTIVE'
      WHERE tenant_id = iv_tenant_id.

    rv_status = 'SUCCESS'.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD on_unsubscribe.
    " 1. Export tenant data for customer (if requested)
    " 2. Drop HDI container (including all data)
    EXECUTE IMMEDIATE |DROP HDI CONTAINER { iv_tenant_id } CASCADE|.
    " 3. Remove tenant from registry
    DELETE FROM ztenant_registry
      WHERE tenant_id = iv_tenant_id.
    " GDPR: ensure personal data is deleted or anonymized
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* HDI container administration:
* SELECT * FROM SYS_DATABASES.M_HDI_CONTAINERS;
* View container status, size, and creation date
* Monitor per-container resource usage

sections.title: CDS DCL for Multi-Tenant Row-Level Security

description.value:
CDS Data Control Language (DCL) is the primary mechanism for implementing row-level security in SAP's multi-tenant data models. While DCL is commonly discussed in the context of PFCG authorization, its application to multi-tenancy is even more foundational: DCL can enforce tenant isolation at the database query level, ensuring that even if a bug in the application code omits the tenant filter, the database layer automatically prevents cross-tenant data access. This is defense-in-depth — the application layer provides tenant context, and the database layer enforces it.

DCL for Tenant Isolation: For a pool-per-tenant design (where all tenants share the same schema), the DCL role includes a condition that the current user's tenant ID matches the tenant_id column in the table. The tenant ID is derived from the user's authentication context (JWT token, SAML assertion, or SAP user master attribute). The DCL role is defined once on the CDS view and applies to all queries — SELECT, UPDATE, DELETE, and INSERT (via the @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK annotation). The key DCL aspect is ASPECT pfcg_auth with a custom authorization field for tenant ID, or a WHERE condition that references the tenant-specific field directly.

Static vs Dynamic DCL: (1) Static DCL: The tenant ID is hardcoded in the DCL role (e.g., WHERE tenant_id = 'ACME'). This is only useful for per-tenant DCL roles — each tenant gets a separate role definition. This is impractical for multi-tenant systems with many tenants. (2) Dynamic DCL: The tenant ID is derived at runtime from the user's login context. This is implemented using the ASPECT pfcg_auth with the authorization object containing the tenant ID field, or by using the cl_abap_typedescr environment to fetch the tenant context. Dynamic DCL is the correct approach for multi-tenant applications — one DCL role applies to all tenants, and the runtime evaluates the current user's tenant context on each query.

Staff+ Considerations for DCL in Multi-Tenant: (1) DCL is transparent to the developer — the WHERE clause injection is invisible in the SQL logs. This can be confusing during debugging: a SELECT statement that works in development (single tenant) may fail in production (multi-tenant) because the DCL silently filters out cross-tenant rows. Always include a tenant_id field in error messages for debugging. (2) DCL adds overhead to every query: the runtime evaluates the DCL role and injects the WHERE condition. For high-throughput queries (thousands per second), benchmark the DCL overhead. In tests, complex DCL with multiple ASPECTs adds 5-15% query overhead. (3) DCL can be bypassed if the consuming service has the @AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED annotation — ensure that all multi-tenant views use #CHECK or #PRIVILEGED_ONLY. (4) For cross-tenant analytics, use a separate role that explicitly allows reading from multiple tenants (with appropriate authorization). The DCL for the analytical role uses OR tenant_id IN (SELECT tenant_id FROM authorized_tenants WHERE user_id = CURRENT_USER).

@EndUserText.label: 'Multi-tenant DCL - enforce tenant isolation'
@MappingRole: true
define role Z_Multitenant_Access {
  grant select on Z_Multitenant_Data
    where
      /*
        Dynamic tenant isolation:
        - tenant_id is a field in the CDS view
        - The current user's tenant ID is derived from PFCG auth
        - SA (System Admin) can see all tenants via wildcard
      */
      tenant_id = asp pfcg_auth('Z_MT_AUTH', 'Z_TENANT_ID', '', '')
        where aspect.mapped(Z_TENANT_ID) = '?'
        /* '?' means any value in the user's auth object is allowed */
      ;

  grant select on Z_Multitenant_Data
    where
      /* System administrator — access to all tenants */
      asp pfcg_auth('S_TCODE', 'TCD', '', '')
        where aspect.mapped(TCD) = 'SM30'
      ;
      /* Note: this is an override role — admin sees everything */
}

* Alternative: Using environment variable in DCL (for BTP)
* Currently not supported natively in DCL syntax,
* but you can use an ABAP helper class:

@EndUserText.label: 'Multi-tenant data with tenant_id'
define view Z_Multitenant_Data
  as select from zmultitenant_data
  association [1..1] to Z_Tenant_Context as _Context
    on $projection.tenant_id = _Context.TenantID
{
  key tenant_id,
  key record_id,
      payload
}
where
  /*
    The where clause is injected by DCL —
    if the DCL is not active, this defaults to empty
    (no data returned), which is the safe default.
  */
  tenant_id = $session.user_tenant_id

* DCL best practices for multi-tenant:
* 1. Always use dynamic DCL (not per-tenant roles)
* 2. Test DCL with multiple user contexts in unit tests
* 3. Add a test for the 'no tenant context' scenario (should fail)
* 4. Monitor DCL overhead via HANA query plan
* 5. Use separate DCL for cross-tenant analytical roles

organizationalDimension.title: Adopting CDS DCL for Multi-Tenant Security Across the Enterprise
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: Start with a DCL governance board (Security, Platform, Legal) that defines tenant-ID propagation rules across the landscape. Run a DCL audit sprint to discover all ABAP programs and CDS views that access tenant-sensitive data. Then roll out DCL policies in waves: core finance views first, logistics next, analytics last.

stakeholders.name: Basis/Security Team
stakeholders.concern: DCL must integrate with existing authorization objects (S_TCODE, S_ADMI_FCD) and not create security gaps
stakeholders.engagement: Co-design DCL templates; run quarterly DCL policy reviews

stakeholders.name: Application Architects
stakeholders.concern: DCL propagation across JOINs and associations between tenant and shared data
stakeholders.engagement: Define DCL inheritance rules via CDS associations; enforce in code reviews

stakeholders.name: Product Owners
stakeholders.concern: DCL errors causing data access issues in existing reports
stakeholders.engagement: Approve DCL change windows; participate in regression test planning

activities: Scan all CDS views for missing DCL
Implement PFCG-compatible DCL for finance/controlling views
Set up CI check that rejects CDS views without DCL
Add DCL for AMDP and table functions
Roll out DCL monitoring via tenant security dashboard

metrics: % of CDS views with DCL coverage (target: 100%)
Tenant data leak incidents per quarter (target: 0)
DCL policy deployment time via CI (target: < 5 minutes)
antiPatterns: Inheriting DCL from standard SAP views without review — may expose restricted tenant data
Hardcoding tenant IDs in DCL literals instead of using session variables
Applying same DCL policy to all tenants when regulatory requirements differ per region

description.value:
One of the hardest problems in multi-tenant architectures is supporting cross-tenant analytics — reporting across multiple tenants for a managed service provider (MSP) or a corporate parent with multiple subsidiaries. The fundamental tension is between data isolation (each tenant's data must be protected) and consolidated visibility (the MSP must see aggregated data across tenants). Solving this requires both architectural patterns and authorization design.

Cross-Tenant Analytics Patterns: (1) Export Warehouse (ETL out): Each tenant's data is periodically exported (daily/hourly) to a separate analytics data warehouse (e.g., SAP BW/4HANA, SAP Data Warehouse Cloud, or Snowflake/BigQuery). The export is performed by a privileged system user that has access to all tenants. In the warehouse, tenant_id is a dimension column, and access to rows is controlled by the warehouse's own authorization model (row-level security in Snowflake, data masking in BigQuery, or analytical authorizations in BW). This pattern provides the strongest isolation because the operational database is never queried directly for analytics. The cost is the ETL infrastructure and data staleness (the warehouse is always behind the operational data by the export interval). (2) Federated Query (query in place): A cross-tenant analytics service queries each tenant's data in place, aggregates the results, and returns them to the requesting user. This requires the analytics service to have authorized access to all tenants. In SAP, this is implemented using the SAP HANA federation capabilities (Virtual Tables or Remote Sources) that connect to each tenant's HDI container or schema. The analytics service has a specific authorization object that allows reading from any tenant's data. The data stays in place (no copy), so it is always current. The disadvantage is query performance: the federation layer must send parallel queries to each tenant and aggregate the results.

Data Lakehouse Pattern for SAP Multi-Tenant Analytics: The modern approach is to stream all tenants' operational data into a data lakehouse (e.g., using SAP CDC (Change Data Capture) from HANA to Kafka to object storage with a query engine on top). Each tenant's data is stored in a partitioned Parquet dataset, with tenant_id as the partition key. The query engine (e.g., Trino, Spark, SAP DataSphere) reads the relevant partitions based on the requesting user's tenant authorization. This gives real-time or near-real-time data (streaming latency of seconds), infinite scalability (object storage is virtually unlimited), and fine-grained access control (partition-level security). This pattern aligns with the data mesh architecture — each tenant owns its data domain, and the analytics layer provides a unified query interface with embedded governance.

* Cross-tenant analytics — SAP BW/4HANA extraction
* Extract data from each tenant using RFC

CLASS zcl_cross_tenant_extract DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: extract_all_tenants
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_data) TYPE zanalytics_data_tab.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    METHODS: get_tenant_list
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_tenants) TYPE ztenant_list_tab,
      extract_tenant
        IMPORTING iv_tenant_id  TYPE string
        RETURNING VALUE(rt_data) TYPE zanalytics_data_tab.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_cross_tenant_extract IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD extract_all_tenants.
    DATA(lt_tenants) = get_tenant_list( ).
    LOOP AT lt_tenants INTO DATA(ls_tenant).
      " Connect to tenant's system via RFC
      DATA(lv_rfc_dest) = |S4H_{ ls_tenant-tenant_id }|.
      CALL FUNCTION 'Z_TENANT_SALES_EXTRACT'
        DESTINATION lv_rfc_dest
        TABLES
          et_sales = DATA(lt_sales).

      " Enrich with tenant ID
      LOOP AT lt_sales ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_sale>).
        <fs_sale>-tenant_id = ls_tenant-tenant_id.
      ENDLOOP.

      APPEND LINES OF lt_sales TO rt_data.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD get_tenant_list.
    SELECT tenant_id, subdomain FROM ztenant_registry
      WHERE status = 'ACTIVE'
      INTO TABLE @rt_tenants.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Cross-tenant DCL for analytical role (special auth):
define role Z_Analytics_CrossTenant {
  grant select on Z_Multitenant_Data
    where
      asp pfcg_auth('Z_ANALYTICS', 'Z_TENANT', '', '')
        where
          " User can see all tenants they have auth for
          tenant_id IN (
            SELECT field_value FROM zuser_tenant_auth
              WHERE user_id = $session.user
          );
}
* This role is assigned to analytics users only
* It does not replace the tenant isolation DCL for operational users

adversarialResponse.title: Cross-Tenant Analytics Without Data Leakage
adversarialResponse.challenge: Executive leadership wants a consolidated BI dashboard showing revenue across all 200 tenants to identify market trends. Each tenant's data must remain isolated per GDPR and contractual SLAs. Current solution: export each tenant's data individually and merge in Excel (16 hours per refresh, stale by day 2).
adversarialResponse.badResponse: Create a new CDS view that UNIONs all tenant tables and grant access to the BI team. It works in dev, but in production the view exposes tenant_ids and allows cross-tenant filtering. The first auditor review flags a GDPR violation; the CEO gets a regulatory fine notice.
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: Implement a privacy-preserving aggregation layer: (1) Create anonymized tenant summary tables that store only KPI aggregates (no tenant_id, no PII). (2) Use HANA's built-in data masking and anonymization (k-anonymity, differential privacy). (3) Schedule daily batch aggregation via AMDP with tenant-scoped execution. (4) Grant BI team access to the aggregation layer only — revoke direct tenant table access. The 16-hour refresh drops to 12 minutes; compliance team approves the architecture.
adversarialResponse.pattern: Aggregation layer pattern: Raw tenant data → privacy-preserving aggregate tables → BI dashboards. The aggregation pipeline runs with tenant-level authorization (each aggregation job runs as the tenant's technical user). Aggregated results lose tenant identity but preserve statistical accuracy. This is the same pattern used by BTP's Data Privacy Integration service and SAP Analytics Cloud's multi-tenant reporting.

sections.title: Multi-Cloud SAP — AWS, GCP, Azure & Data Sovereignty

description.value:
Large enterprises increasingly deploy SAP across multiple cloud providers — AWS for production S/4HANA, GCP for analytics (BigQuery, Vertex AI consuming SAP data), and Azure for Microsoft-integrated workloads (Teams, Office 365, Power BI connected to SAP). This multi-cloud SAP architecture is driven by: (1) avoiding vendor lock-in, (2) leveraging each cloud's unique capabilities (GCP for AI/ML, AWS for HANA-optimized instances, Azure for Microsoft 365 integration), (3) data sovereignty requirements (data must stay within specific geographic regions), and (4) acquisition integration (acquired companies may already be on different clouds). Managing multi-tenancy across clouds adds another layer of complexity: tenant data must be isolated not just within a cloud but across clouds.

Multi-Cloud Tenant Isolation: When tenants are deployed across clouds, each tenant's data resides in a specific cloud provider's infrastructure. The tenant-to-cloud mapping is determined by: (1) Data residency: EU customer data must stay in EU-based cloud regions (Frankfurt, Ireland, Paris). Under GDPR, a tenant from Germany cannot have data stored in US-based cloud regions. The tenant's data must be pinned to the EU region. (2) Performance: Tenants with users in Asia-Pacific should be deployed in AWS Tokyo or GCP Singapore to minimize latency. (3) Cost: Different cloud providers offer different pricing for HANA-optimized instances. A cost optimization layer can periodically evaluate whether specific tenants should be moved to a different cloud provider for cost savings. The tenant migration across clouds (move from AWS to GCP) requires a multi-step process: replicate the tenant's HDI container or HANA schema to the target cloud, validate the data, switch the DNS or API gateway routing, and decommission the source.

Data Sovereignty Compliance: Data sovereignty laws (GDPR in Europe, CCPA in California, LGPD in Brazil, China's Data Security Law) mandate that personal data must be stored and processed within the country or region of origin. For multi-tenant SAP platforms, this means: (1) The tenant provisioning process must capture the data residency requirement (specified by the customer during onboarding). (2) The tenant's data must be stored only in cloud regions that comply with the residency requirement. (3) Cross-region data transfer (e.g., for disaster recovery or analytics) must use approved mechanisms (Standard Contractual Clauses, Binding Corporate Rules). (4) The tenant's data must be encryptable at rest and in-transit using customer-managed keys (CMEK/CMK in GCP/AWS/Azure). The Staff+ pattern is to design a data residency layer that abstracts the cloud region selection: the onboarding service selects the appropriate cloud region based on the tenant's residency requirements and provisions the infrastructure accordingly. The routing layer ensures that all API calls for that tenant are directed to the correct cloud region.

* Cloud region selection based on data residency
CLASS zcl_data_residency DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_region_config,
             country_code  TYPE string,
             cloud_provider TYPE string,
             region         TYPE string,
             is_allowed     TYPE abap_bool,
           END OF ty_region_config.

    CLASS-METHODS: get_allowed_regions
      IMPORTING iv_country_code   TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_regions)  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_region_config,
      validate_tenant_region
        IMPORTING iv_tenant_id   TYPE string
                  iv_region     TYPE string
        RETURNING VALUE(rv_valid) TYPE abap_bool.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_data_residency IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD get_allowed_regions.
    " EU countries -> only EU regions
    CASE iv_country_code.
      WHEN 'DE' OR 'FR' OR 'IT' OR 'ES' OR 'NL' OR 'BE'.
        " GDPR: data must stay in EU
        rt_regions = VALUE #(
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'AWS'   region = 'eu-central-1'    is_allowed = abap_true )
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'GCP'   region = 'europe-west1'    is_allowed = abap_true )
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'Azure' region = 'westeurope'      is_allowed = abap_true )
        ).
      WHEN 'US'.
        rt_regions = VALUE #(
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'AWS'   region = 'us-east-1'  is_allowed = abap_true )
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'GCP'   region = 'us-central1' is_allowed = abap_true )
        ).
      WHEN 'BR'.
        " LGPD: data must stay in Brazil
        rt_regions = VALUE #(
          ( country_code = iv_country_code
            cloud_provider = 'AWS' region = 'sa-east-1' is_allowed = abap_true )
        ).
      WHEN OTHERS.
        " Default to any region
        rt_regions = VALUE #( ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD validate_tenant_region.
    DATA(lt_allowed) = get_allowed_regions(
      iv_country_code = zcl_cloud_tenant_context=>get_tenant_country(
        iv_tenant_id = iv_tenant_id ) ).

    rv_valid = line_exists( lt_allowed[
      region = iv_region ] ).
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK) setup
* Each tenant gets their own KMS key
* HANA Cloud: ALTER SYSTEM SET ENCRYPTION KEY
*   FOR TENANT <tenant_id> USING <kms_key_arn>
* AWS: AWS KMS key per tenant
* GCP: Cloud KMS key ring per tenant
* Azure: Azure Key Vault key per tenant

quantifiedTradeoff.title: Multi-Cloud vs Single Cloud for SAP Tenant Deployment

variables.name: R
variables.description: Revenue at risk if cloud outage blocks tenant operations (per-hour)

variables.name: MTTR_single
variables.description: Mean time to recover single-cloud outage (hours)

variables.name: MTTR_multi
variables.description: Mean time to failover to second cloud (hours)

variables.name: P_outage
variables.description: Annual probability of major cloud outage

calculation.formula: Annual cost of multi-cloud = N × C_multi × 12. Annual cost of single-cloud = N × C_single × 12. Expected annual outage cost = P_outage × R × MTTR × N_affected_tenants.
calculation.ap: 200 × $500 × 12 = $1,200,000 (single-cloud annually) + 0.02 × $50,000 × 4 × 200 = $800,000 expected outage loss = $2,000,000 total expected annual cost
calculation.breakeven: Multi-cloud is cheaper when P_outage × MTTR × R > C_multi − C_single per tenant. Here: 0.02 × 4 × $50,000 = $4,000 > $300 = $800 − $500. Multi-cloud saves $80,000/year expected. But if outage probability is 0.5% or MTTR is under 1 hour, single-cloud wins.

details: Staff+ Insight: Multi-tenancy expertise is directly transferable from SAP to any SaaS platform. FAANG companies (especially Google with Workspace, AWS with Control Tower, Meta with Business Tools) all operate multi-tenant platforms at global scale. The fundamental trade-offs — isolation vs cost, schema-per-tenant vs shared-everything — are universal. Demonstrating deep understanding of tenant isolation models, HDI containers, DCL for row-level security, and cross-tenant analytics positions you as a true platform architect, not just an SAP engineer. Data sovereignty in multi-cloud SAP is a topic that directly maps to Google Cloud's data residency and sovereign controls offerings.
